message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Prior studies have documented reliable associations between SARS-CoV-2 infection and adverse cognitive impact in older adults. The current study sought to determine whether SARS-CoV-2 infection and COVID-19 symptom severity are associated with cognitive dysfunction among young adults and middled-aged adults in the general population.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Method</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    The Canadian COVID-19 Experiences Project (CCEP) survey involves 1,958 adults with equal representation of vaccinated and vaccine hesitant adults between the ages of 18 and 54 years. The sample comprised 1,958 adults with a mean age of 37 years (
                    <jats:italic>SD</jats:italic>
                    =10.4); 60.8% were female. The primary outcome was symptoms of cognitive dysfunction assessed via an abbreviated form of the Barkley Deficits in Executive Functioning Scale (BDEFS) and performance on a validated decision-making task.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Young and middle-aged adults with a positive SARS-CoV-2 infection history reported a significantly higher number of symptoms of executive dysfunction (
                    <jats:italic>M</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sub>
                      <jats:italic>adj</jats:italic>
                    </jats:sub>
                    =1.89,
                    <jats:italic>SE</jats:italic>
                    =0.08,
                    <jats:italic>CI</jats:italic>
                    : 1.74, 2.04;
                    <jats:italic>n</jats:italic>
                    =175) than their non-infected counterparts (
                    <jats:italic>M</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sub>
                      <jats:italic>adj</jats:italic>
                    </jats:sub>
                    =1.63,
                    <jats:italic>SE</jats:italic>
                    =0.08,
                    <jats:italic>CI</jats:italic>
                    : 1.47,1.80;
                    <jats:italic>n</jats:italic>
                    =1,599; β=0.26,
                    <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                    =.001). Among those infected, there was a dose-response relationship between COVID-19 symptom severity and level of executive dysfunction, with moderate (β=0.23,
                    <jats:italic>CI</jats:italic>
                    : 0.003-0.46) and very/extremely severe (β= 0.69,
                    <jats:italic>CI</jats:italic>
                    : 0.22-1.16) COVID-19 symptoms being associated with significantly greater dysfunction, compared to asymptomatic. These effects remained reliable and of similar magnitude after controlling for age, sex, vaccination status, income, and geographic region, and after removal of those who had been intubated during hospitalization. Similar effects were found for the decision-making task.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Positive SARS-CoV-2 infection history and COVID-19 symptom severity are associated with executive dysfunction among young and middle-aged adults with no history of medically induced coma. These findings are evident on self-reported and task-related indicators of cognitive function.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Executive dysfunction following SARS-CoV-2 infection: A cross-sectional examination in a population-representative sample

reference.unstructured: Hall P , Fong G , Hitchman S. The Canadian COVID-19 Experiences Survey: Study Protocol. medRxiv. Published online 2021.

Canada PHA of. COVID-19 daily epidemiology update. aem. Published April 19, 2020. Accessed January 2, 2022. https://health-infobase.canada.ca/covid-19/epidemiological-summary-covid-19-cases.html?stat=rate&measure=total_last14&map=pt
Barkley RA . Barkley Deficits in Executive Functioning Scale (BDEFS). Guilford Press; 2011.
